items.title: Wike: The standout FCT minister in 50 years of Abuja
items.description: By KEHINDE OLAOSEBIKAN I was there. The President said it. A former FCT minister affirmed it. As a resident since 1988, I confirm it. I was at the Bola Ahmed Tinubu International Conference Centre, Area 11, Garki, Abuja, Friday night for the special thanksgiving banquet organized to celebrate the successful inauguration of 31 projects across the Federal Capital Territory as part of activities marking the third anniversary of the present administration. Speaking through the President of the Senate, Senator Godswill Akpabio, the President, Asiwaju Bola Ahmed Tinubu, declared the current FCT minister, Barrister Nyesom Wike, the best minister so far in the 50 years’ existence of the territory. Minutes earlier, the Vice President, Senator Kashim Shettima, spoke through Dr. Aliyu Modibo. Dr. Modibo, who served as FCT minister between 2007 and 2008, is currently the Special Adviser to the President on General Duties in the office of the Vice President. Speaking ex tempore, Modibo informed the audience of eminent Nigerians that he had the VP’s permission to do “a peer review of all the past and present FCT ministers” on the occasion. His verdict was clear: the incumbent is “one of the best three ministers” in the history of the administration of the 8,000 square kilometre territory. He named the three as Air Vice Marshal Hamza Abdullahi, Major General Muhammadu Gado Nasko and the incumbent, Barrister Nyesom Wike. Dr. Modibo, however, left the choice of who is first, second or third to the audience. But the gathering, made up of serving and past ministers, past and serving members of the National Assembly, serving and former permanent secretaries, serving and former directors of the Ministry of the Federal Capital Territory, serving and past chairmen of FCT area councils and others, chorused: “Wike is the first,” and Modibo smiled heartily. Two endorsements: one from the President, one from a peer who has sat in the same chair. That is weighty. Thirty eight years of watching, I write as a witness. I have lived in Abuja since 1988. I was Vanguard’s pioneer Abuja bureau chief and worked in that capacity and in higher positions for many years. In 38 years, I have watched 12 of the 17 ministers who have run the FCT. From Chief Mobolaji Ajose Adeogun (1976-79), John Jatau Kadiya (1979-83), Iro Abubakar Dan Musa (1982-83), Haliru Dantoro (1983), Major General Mamman Jiya Vatsa (1984-85), Major General Hamza Abdullahi (1986-89), Major General Gado Nasko (1989-93), Major General Mamman Kontagora (1998-99), Ibrahim Bunu (1999-2001), Mohammed Abba Gana (2001-2003), Nasir El Rufai (2003-2007), Aliyu Modibo (2007-2008), Muhammed Adamu Aliero (2008-2010), Senator Bala Abubakar Mohammed (2010-2015), Mohammed Musa Bello (2015-2023) to the incumbent, Barrister Nyesom Wike, I have seen masterplans launched and abandoned. I have seen satellite towns promised and ignored. I have seen a beautiful Central Area surrounded by benighted communities. So, when the President spoke, I understood the context. The Wike difference What has changed? Three things, and they are visible. Speed. Projects that gathered dust for 10 years are now being commissioned every quarter. The Outer Ring Road. Arterial roads in Abuja Municipal, Gwagwalada, Kuje, Bwari, Kwali. Contractors are back because payments are coming. Equity. For 50 years, Abuja meant the Central Area, Garki 1 and 2, Wuse 1 and 2, Maitama, Asokoro and partially Kubwa, Karu, Nyanya and a few other places. Wike is taking asphalt, light and water to the various other districts. The 8,000 sq km territory is finally being treated as one city. On this, I recall vividly that the recurring question at the yearly ministerial briefings by journalists to FCT ministers was the need to spread development beyond the city centre. Top journalists like Olu Akerele of Concord, Ibrahim Biu of New Nigerian, Idahosa Asowata of Daily Times, Harris Dawurang of Standard, Hadiza Abdullahi of Triumph, Nike Adelegan of Punch, Camillus Eboh of The Guardian, Jide Olawuyi of Sketch, Dapo Olufade of Vanguard, Innocent Nwobodo of Champion, Osita Izunaso of Satellite, Segun Adeniyi of African Concord, Anselm Okolo and Ola Awoniyi of Tribune, Razak Magaji of Newswatch, Sunday Dare of The News, Sherif Muhammed of Democrat, Felix Onuah of The Reporter, Taweh Zakah of New Nigerian, Ebun Obadofin, Rasheed Bello, Muhammed Adamu of Concord, Tunde Rahman of Times, Martins Oloja, Alex Kabba, Alifa Daniel of Abuja Newsday, my humble self and others whose names now escape me repeatedly advised the ministers to pay attention to the satellite towns even as the city centre was being rapidly developed. We warned against repeating Lagos’s uneven development pattern. As the Fourth Estate, we sounded the alarm early. Order. Renewed enforcement of the Abuja master plan. Clean up of parks and green areas. Logistics for security agencies. Abuja is beginning to look and feel like a capital again. You can now truly see Abuja and marvel. This is delivery at scale, and at pace. Leadership with humility But the moment that defined the night was Wike’s response. He did not take a victory lap. He was fair to his predecessors. “All of them were good with great ideas,” he said. “What they lacked were visionary leaders who could motivate them to give their best.” Then he pointed to the source of his drive: “I am privileged, over privileged, to serve as FCT minister under President Bola Ahmed Tinubu – the best leader, motivator, mentor, enabler, inspiration any minister could dream of.” He gives credit up, shares credit sideways, owns the delivery. That is leadership. Why the President is right Abuja is Nigeria’s face. When an investor lands at Nnamdi Azikiwe International Airport, his first impression of Nigeria is the FCT. For decades we built a capital for photo ops and neglected a capital for people. Wike is closing that gap. He is connecting Abuja. He is securing Abuja. He is lighting up Abuja. I have no brief to praise. As a journalist and resident, my job is to record what I see. And what I see is unprecedented delivery in under three years. The President’s declaration is not flattery. It is fact – backed by a peer review from a former FCT minister, and by the reality on our roads every day. Fifty years. 17 ministers. One stands out. President Tinubu is right. Barrister Nyesom Wike is the standout minister in the history of the Federal Capital Territory. •Olaosebikan is the pioneer Abuja bureau chief of Vanguard Newspapers. The post Wike: The standout FCT minister in 50 years of Abuja appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: Souveraineté énergétique : Le Burkina Faso inaugure une centrale thermique de 50 MW à Pabré
items.description: Le Premier ministre, Chef du Gouvernement, le Camarade Rimtalba Jean Emmanuel Ouédraogo, a procédé, ce mardi 21 juillet 2026 à Pabré, à l’inauguration officielle de la centrale thermique d’urgence d’une capacité de 50 mégawatts. ‎Réalisée en seulement 90 jours, contre un délai contractuel de 120 jours, cette infrastructure, d’un coût de 18 milliards de FCFA, vient renforcer les capacités de production de la Société nationale d’électricité du Burkina (SONABEL) et contribuer à l’amélioration de l’offre énergétique nationale. ‎La cérémonie est intervenue une heure après la pose de la première pierre de la future centrale thermique de Ouaga Sud-Est, dans la commune de Saaba, par le Camarade ministre de l’Énergie, des Mines et des Carrières, Yacouba Zabré Gouba. D’une capacité projetée de 120 mégawatts, cette nouvelle infrastructure nécessitera un investissement estimé à 133 milliards de FCFA. ‎Le Premier ministre a salué la mise en service de la centrale de Pabré, premier jalon d’un vaste programme engagé dans le cadre de la Révolution progressiste populaire, en vue d’assurer l’autonomie stratégique et la souveraineté énergétique du Burkina Faso. ‎« Aujourd’hui, la souveraineté du Burkina Faso est également en marche dans le domaine de l’énergie. Nous n’aurons de repos que lorsque nous aurons réussi à faire en sorte que la production nationale d’électricité soit supérieure à la demande », a-t-il soutenu. ‎Le Chef du Gouvernement a relevé que les choix opérés par le passé, principalement fondés sur l’importation de l’électricité, ont montré leurs limites, en exposant le pays aux aléas extérieurs et aux perturbations de l’approvisionnement énergétique. ‎« Notre pays avait fait le choix de mettre l’accent sur l’importation de l’énergie. Cette option a montré ses limites, parce que nous restions tributaires de la qualité de la production des pays d’où nous importions l’électricité. Aujourd’hui, nous avons décidé de construire nos propres capacités de production pour aller vers l’autonomie stratégique et la souveraineté énergétique », a-t-il déclaré. ‎Dans son discours, le ministre de l’Énergie, des Mines et des Carrières, le Camarade Yacouba Zabré Gouba, a souligné que la mise en service de cette centrale va bien au-delà d’un simple investissement dans le secteur de l’énergie. ‎Selon le ministre, cette réalisation traduit la volonté du Gouvernement de bâtir progressivement l’indépendance énergétique du pays, conformément à la vision portée par le Président du Faso, Chef de l’État, le Camarade Capitaine Ibrahim Traoré. Cette ambition répond à un impératif de souveraineté nationale, de développement économique et d’amélioration des conditions de vie des populations. ‎Au nom de l’entreprise Al Ahram Taqa Burkina, son Administrateur général, Massoud Hassan Abdo Jaied, a exprimé sa gratitude aux autorités burkinabè, à la SONABEL ainsi qu’à l’ensemble des partenaires ayant contribué à la réalisation du projet. ‎Il a réaffirmé l’engagement de son groupe à accompagner durablement le Burkina Faso dans sa marche vers la souveraineté énergétique, rappelant que l’électricité constitue un levier essentiel pour le développement de l’industrie, de l’agriculture, des mines, de l’éducation et des services publics. ‎Au cours de la cérémonie, le Président-directeur général et l’Administrateur général de l’entreprise Al Ahram Taqa ont été élevés au grade de Chevalier de l’Ordre du mérite burkinabè, en reconnaissance de leur contribution au développement du secteur énergétique national. ‎La centrale thermique de Pabré s’inscrit dans un vaste programme national qui prévoit prochainement l’inauguration de nouvelles centrales à Bobo-Dioulasso, Koudougou, Kaya, Komsilga et Toécé, pour une capacité additionnelle globale de plus de 220 mégawatts. ‎À travers ces investissements, le Gouvernement entend accroître durablement les capacités nationales de production d’électricité, répondre à la demande croissante en énergie et soutenir le développement économique et social du Burkina Faso. Source : ‎𝐃𝐂𝐑𝐏/𝐏𝐫𝐢𝐦𝐚𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞 L’article Souveraineté énergétique : Le Burkina Faso inaugure une centrale thermique de 50 MW à Pabré est apparu en premier sur NetAfrique.net .

items.title: Keeping exams within reach of every child
items.description: The Federal Government did well to suspend its proposed increase in the registration fees for the West African Examinations Council (WAEC) and the National Examinations Council (NECO) examinations. It is a decision that reflects not only sensitivity to public opinion but also a recognition of the harsh economic realities confronting Nigerian families. We are calling for an indefinite suspension of the proposal, and the reasons for this are obvious. This is hardly the time to impose additional financial burdens on citizens. Across the country, households are battling soaring food prices, rising transport fares, higher electricity tariffs, and escalating healthcare costs. Removal of the fuel subsidy and other economic reforms, however well-intentioned, have significantly eroded purchasing power, leaving many families struggling to provide even the basic necessities of life. In such circumstances, increasing the cost of public examinations would have been both unfair and insensitive. Education is not a luxury to be enjoyed only by those who can afford it. It is a fundamental public good and the surest pathway to national development. Every child who completes secondary education and obtains the necessary qualifications becomes a potential contributor to economic growth, social stability, and national development. Public policy should therefore remove barriers to education rather than create new ones. For thousands of families, particularly those in rural communities and low-income urban settlements, the suspended fee increase represented far more than another routine price rise. It threatened to deny many deserving students the opportunity to sit examinations they had spent years preparing for. It would have been a cruel irony for a child to complete secondary school only to be prevented from obtaining certification because of the exorbitant cost of exams. Government’s role in education is not to maximise revenue or recover every operational cost from citizens. Public education exists because an educated population benefits the entire nation. Examinations conducted by public bodies should therefore remain affordable, with government providing the necessary support to bridge funding gaps. Where rising operational costs genuinely exist, they should be addressed through budgetary allocations, greater efficiency, prudent financial management, and targeted subsidies rather than by transferring every burden to already distressed parents. Nigeria already faces the daunting challenge of millions of out-of-school children. Policies that make education more expensive can only worsen that crisis, deepen inequality, and widen the gap between the privileged and the disadvantaged. A nation cannot build a prosperous future while erecting financial obstacles before its young people. The suspension of the proposed increase is a welcome victory for common sense. It should not become a temporary pause before it returns in another guise. Government should vigorously pursue policies that make education more accessible, affordable, and inclusive. The country’s greatest investment is not in higher examination fees but in the limitless potential of its children. The post Keeping exams within reach of every child appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: What are the functions of ministries in a country? (Part 3), by Usman Sarki
items.description: “The task of leadership is not to put greatness into humanity, but to elicit it, for the greatness is already there ”, John Buchan. In the hilarious and admirable British comedy series, “Yes, Prime Minister”, Dorothy Wainwright, an adviser to the fictional Prime Minister James Hacker, once delivered a devastating verdict on the Department of Education. According to her, it was not the guardian of education but its graveyard. Her remedy was as brutal as it was mirthful: “Get rid of it! Abolish it! Remove it! Expunge it! Eliminate it! Eradicate it! Exterminate it! Get rid of it!” By her logic and the extension of that reasoning, the Department of Industry could be viewed as the graveyard of industry, the Department of Health as the graveyard of health, and so on. What began as comedy thus became a savage indictment of bureaucracy, institutional pretence and the capacity of government departments to survive long after the purposes for which they were created have been defeated. Comedy, although frowned upon by the venerable Jorge in Umberto Eco’s “The Name of the Rose”, is, when properly understood, often a higher form of political philosophy and criticism. It exaggerates and mocks reality in order to expose it. It makes us laugh, but only because the truth is sometimes too painful, too dangerous or too inconvenient to be stated plainly. Upon serious reflection, therefore, I have often wondered whether we should transport Dorothy Wainwright’s logic from British comedy to the real-life situation of Nigeria, where we have dozens of ministries, hundreds of departments and thousands of agencies, yet without visible, consistent and measurable improvement in the lives, fortunes and prospects of the people. What, indeed, are the functions of ministries in a country? Are they merely administrative contraptions created to distribute offices, titles, vehicles, allowances and political patronage? Are they instruments of policy formulation and implementation? Are they centres of technical competence, national planning and public service delivery? Or have they become, in our peculiar circumstances, ritualistic monuments to the presence of government without the substance of governance? What indeed is the purpose of a state bureaucracy? In a properly ordered state, ministries exist because the responsibilities of government have to be organised, allocated, specialised and executed through defined institutions. The Ministry of Education should exist because the nation must educate its children, train its teachers, regulate its schools and improve learning outcomes. The Ministry of Health should exist because the state must protect life, prevent disease, organise hospitals and raise standards of public health. The Ministry of Works should exist because roads, bridges and public infrastructure cannot be left to chance. Likewise, the Ministries of Agriculture and Food Security, Livestock Development and Water Resources should exist because water and food security, meat and dairy production, irrigation, extension services and value chains are matters of national survival. In other words, a ministry is not supposed to be an ornamental structure, a mere nameplate on a building, or a waiting room for politicians, consultants, contractors and petitioners. It is an instrument through which public purpose is translated into organised action. Its legitimacy lies not in its budgetary allocation, ministerial convoy, official stationery or ceremonial protocol, but in the results it produces for the people. This is where the Nigerian problem begins. We have become a country of administrative proliferation without corresponding performance. Every difficulty seems to be answered by creating another ministry, department, agency, commission, board, committee or task force. We confuse multiplication with seriousness, expansion with effectiveness, and bureaucracy with governance. Whenever a new concern arises, our instinct is not first to ask whether existing institutions can be made to work. It is to create another institution, appoint another leadership, rent another office, procure another fleet of vehicles and add another layer to the already crowded architecture of government. Yet, after all this, the basic questions still remain unanswered. If we have ministries of education, why are millions of children still out of school or poorly taught, and why is there a shortage of properly trained and highly motivated teachers? If we have ministries of health, why do hospitals lack basic equipment, drugs, personnel and dignity? If we have ministries of works, why are many roads death traps? If we have ministries of agriculture, why is food increasingly beyond the reach of ordinary families? If we have ministries of power, why does darkness remain one of the most constant features of our national existence? If we have a ministry of livestock, why are meat and dairy products becoming more expensive and less available? The same questions must be asked of other ministries and agencies whose responsibilities require constant interrogation in a fast-changing world. What exactly is the Ministry of Humanitarian Affairs expected to do in a country where poverty, displacement and vulnerability have become permanent features of national life? Is it an emergency response institution, a poverty alleviation vehicle, a social protection platform, or another vast administrative channel through which compassion is converted into bureaucracy? What is the precise task of the Ministry of Women Affairs in the twenty-first century? Is it to organise ceremonies and issue statements, or to transform the material conditions of women through education, health, enterprise, legal protection and participation in national development? What is the function of a ministry responsible for youth in a country where the majority of the population is young, restless, hopeless, underemployed and increasingly alienated from the formal structures of the state? Is it merely to gather youths into conferences and empowerment programmes, or to coordinate a serious national strategy on skills, innovation, productivity, citizenship and opportunity? What are the roles of Ministries of Culture and Sports in a country so richly endowed with talent, languages, heritage, music, film, literature and athletic promise? Are they ornamental sectors for festivals and tournaments, or strategic instruments of identity, national cohesion, creative industry, employment and soft power? The same scrutiny should apply to NEMA, its counterparts in the states, and to ministries responsible for information and aviation. In an age of floods, fires, epidemics, displacement, climate stress and insecurity, emergency management cannot be reduced to the distribution of relief materials after disasters have occurred. It must become a system of prevention, preparedness, early warning, coordination and recovery. Likewise, a Ministry of Information information in the twenty-first century cannot remain a mere loudspeaker for government, nor justify its existence merely by the supervision of NTA and Radio Nigeria. In an age of digital communication, artificial intelligence, misinformation, public distrust and global competition for narratives, it must demonstrate value through enlightened and objective public communication, civic education, transparency, national image making and democratic accountability. A similar query must be posed to the Ministry of Aviation. What exactly is its strategic purpose in a country that does not have a national carrier, struggles to regulate airline operations effectively, and seems unable to bring sanity, affordability and predictability to the cost of air travel? Is it merely an administrative overseer of airports and agencies, or should it be a serious institution for safety regulation, aviation infrastructure, air connectivity, consumer protection, national competitiveness and the strategic positioning of Nigeria as an aviation hub, which in our region, has been appropriated already by Togo and Ghana? These questions are not asked in anger but in genuine bewilderment, sorrow, curiosity and civic responsibility. A country must periodically interrogate its institutions. It must know whether money spent in the name of governance actually produces governance. The tragedy of many ministries in Nigeria is not that they do nothing at all. It is that they often do many things without transforming anything. They hold meetings, produce communiqués, organise retreats, conduct workshops, prepare reports, receive delegations, inaugurate committees, inspect projects, travel abroad and issue press releases. These activities create the appearance of motion. But motion is not the same as movement in the developmental sense. Activity is not the same as achievement. Expenditure is not the same as development. The deeper problem is that ministries have gradually lost the discipline of measurable purpose. Their mandates are often vague, overlapping or duplicated. Their work is compromised by political interference, poor planning, meagre funding, inadequate technical expertise, personality grandstanding, weak monitoring and the absence of consequences for failure. In some cases, the ministry becomes subordinate to the minister’s preferences rather than the nation’s priorities. In others, permanent bureaucracies become custodians of inertia, resisting change while perfecting the rituals of delay. This, perhaps, is why the question of ministries must be taken beyond the formal language of administration into the harder terrain of performance, delivery and national consequence. The post What are the functions of ministries in a country? (Part 3), by Usman Sarki appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: Ferguson says Keegan built Newcastle’s greatest team
items.description: Former Manchester United manager Sir Alex Ferguson said Kevin Keegan “carried the love of the game on his sleeve” as he paid a fond tribute to his former rival. Keegan’s death aged 75 was announced on Monday and Ferguson, recalling their managerial battles, praised the former Newcastle United manager for the impact he made with the Tyneside club. “I had the utmost respect for him,” Ferguson, whose team famously beat Newcastle to the Premier League title in 1995-96, told Track Radio. “We had many duels when he was manager of Newcastle, but I have to say the job he did at Newcastle was fantastic.” Keegan’s first stint as Magpies boss was between 1992 and 1997. He guided the club to promotion to the Premier League in 1992-93 before they went on to finish as runners-up to United three years later. “He re-energised that club and he brought that enthusiasm and fanaticism that great supporters can give. That’s the job he did,” said Ferguson. “And he also brought three great players to the club – [David] Ginola, [Faustino] Asprilla, Philippe Albert – and added a bit of class to the team, and I must say the challenges we had with Newcastle at that time at United were huge. “Kevin carried the love of the game on his sleeve, there’s no question about that, and that carried right on to the fans, to the players. “That period at Newcastle was of emotion, it was excitement. They brought these great players to the club too and they added to it, and it gave Newcastle – I think – their best ever team.” Keegan famously said “I will love it if we beat them” in a TV outburst aimed at Ferguson as Newcastle gave up a 12-point lead when they were beaten to the title by United in 1996. That reaction came after Ferguson questioned whether Leeds and Nottingham Forest would try as hard against Newcastle as they would against Manchester United. Ferguson recalled that the pair bumped into each other at Wentworth Golf Club a few weeks after the end of the season and there was no animosity as they spent time together. “The great thing about him, he held no grudges. That was a fantastic quality he had and we had a great night,” said Ferguson. “All aside whatever happened in previous weeks, that was soon forgotten. I really respected him for that.” ‘Football has lost one of its true greats’ Ferguson joined the floods of tributes being paid to former England captain and manager Keegan, who enjoyed a stellar playing career which involved spells at Scunthorpe United, Liverpool, Hamburg, Southampton and Newcastle. He claimed three league titles, the European Cup, two Uefa Cups and the FA Cup during a six-year spell from 1971 to 1977 at Liverpool. He helped Hamburg win the Bundesliga and earned back-to-back Ballons d’Or in 1978 and 1979. Keegan remains one of only four English players to have won the award to date. As a manager, he was also in charge at Fulham and Manchester City in addition to England and Newcastle. Keegan signed Alan Shearer for a world record £15m fee for Newcastle in 1996 and also took charge of England at a time when the striker was captaining his country. “He was my friend. A man everyone loved. For his passion. For his energy but most of all for his friendship. He loved football and he loved Newcastle more than anything,” said Shearer. “I’m so honoured to have known him. We will miss you Kevin.” Newcastle United’s current manager Eddie Howe got in touch with Keegan when he was appointed by the Magpies in 2021. “Kevin was an inspiration to me as a young player and, I’m honoured to say, a friend whose guidance and support meant a great deal in recent years,” said Howe. “He showed me incredible warmth and generosity. He had already given so much to this football club, yet he immediately made me feel welcome. “Kevin understood better than most the privilege and responsibility of leading Newcastle United. He was always willing to share his wisdom, offer encouragement and provide support, and I will always be grateful for that.” Former Scotland and Liverpool forward Sir Kenny Dalglish described Keegan as “one of the very best players of his generation” who “wore Liverpool’s famous number seven shirt with great distinction”. “His achievements on the pitch were exceptional, but he was also a wonderful person and genuine gentleman,” added Dalglish, who was signed from Celtic to replace Keegan at Liverpool in 1977, and later also succeeded him as manager of Newcastle. “He gave so many people wonderful memories and his teams played the game in a way that supporters loved to watch. “Football has lost one of its true greats.” Phil Thompson described his former England and Liverpool team-mate as the “best of all the people I have known in football and in sport”. “He was a lovely man and that is above all his footballing exploits,” said former defender Thompson. “Humility poured out of Kevin’s veins.” He added: “When he came to Liverpool, he was a star waiting to happen. We got a superhero in 1971 and he was a revelation. He was inspirational.” Former Liverpool forward David Fairclough made his first-team debut for Liverpool in 1975 and said he “looked up” to Keegan and “started to do things the Kevin Keegan way”. “I remember going home after training, having been an apprentice, and telling my friends how close I had been to Kevin Keegan,” Fairclough told BBC Radio Merseyside. “I would tell them about the fact he had even addressed me. “As a young kid, it was so nice that he had noticed me. I remember my mates being gobsmacked about it because he was the top man at Liverpool at that time.” Keegan scored 21 goals in 63 games for England between 1972 and 1982, and went on to manage them from February 1999 until October 2000.

items.title: Viol sur mineurs au Burkina : Le Ministre de la Justice exige la tolérance zéro avec des peines maximales pour les prédateurs d’enfants
items.description: Face à une recrudescence « préoccupante » des violences sexuelles sur mineurs, le gouvernement burkinabè durcit radicalement sa politique pénale. Dans une circulaire datée du 17 juillet 2026, le Ministre de la Justice et des Droits humains, Maître Edasso Rodrigue Bayala, a instruit les parquets de ne plus recourir aux mécanismes de médiation ou de peines alternatives pour les auteurs de viols sur mineurs. Entre injonctions de réquérir les peines maximales et obligation d'appel systématique contre les jugements jugés trop cléments, cette directive marque un tournant sécuritaire et judiciaire majeur pour la protection de l'intégrité physique et psychologique des enfants. La récréation est terminée pour les auteurs de crimes sexuels sur les enfants. C’est le message sans équivoque que vient de lancer la chancellerie à l'ensemble des procureurs du pays. Si le Code de procédure pénale privilégiait jusqu'ici certains mécanismes alternatifs pour une justice dite « réparatrice », la réalité du terrain a imposé une révision brutale de cette stratégie. Le diagnostic du ministre Edasso Rodrigue Bayala est lucide : les juridictions font face à une montée inquiétante des cas de viols sur mineurs. Plus grave encore, le ministre déplore que certains auteurs aient pu bénéficier de médiations pénales ou de peines de faible sévérité, déconnectées de la gravité des faits. Cette situation, selon la circulaire, non seulement « affaiblit la portée dissuasive » de la loi, mais entame profondément la confiance des citoyens envers l'institution judiciaire. Pour corriger ces dérives, le ministre impose désormais aux procureurs quatre orientations strictes : L’interdiction totale de recourir à la médiation ou à la composition pénale pour les viols sur mineurs. L'exclusion des travaux d'intérêt général comme peine de substitution pour ces crimes. Le requis de la peine maximale prévue par la loi dès que les circonstances le justifient. L’appel systématique et sans délai contre toute décision de justice dont la peine paraît manifestement insuffisante au regard des faits ou pour prévenir la récidive. Cette offensive judiciaire ne se veut pas seulement répressive, elle se veut protectrice. En rappelant la « particulière vulnérabilité » des victimes mineures, le ministère de la Justice replace l'intérêt supérieur de l'enfant au cœur de l'action publique. Maître Bayala invite personnellement les membres du ministère public à veiller à ce que leurs réquisitions traduisent cette priorité nationale. À travers cette circulaire, la justice burkinabè espère envoyer un signal de fermeté aux agresseurs tout en garantissant aux familles une réponse pénale à la hauteur du traumatisme subi. L’article Viol sur mineurs au Burkina : Le Ministre de la Justice exige la tolérance zéro avec des peines maximales pour les prédateurs d’enfants est apparu en premier sur NetAfrique.net .

items.title: Nelson Mandela and ANC’s many failures, by Ochereome Nnanna
items.description: If you ask anyone, “who is the greatest African of all time?”, the name of Nelson Mandela of South Africa will likely pop up. I took this question to three Artificial Intelligence (AI) platforms: ChatGPT, Gemini and Claude. They all chorused “Dr Nelson Mandela.” While Gemini and Claude held Kwame Nkrumah in second place with Mansa Musa third, ChatGPT put Mansa Musa second and Nkrumah third. In typical fashion, ChatGPT went on to rank six other greats: Julius Nyerere, Patrice Lumumba, Haile Selassie, Thomas Sankara, Shaka Zulu and Nnamdi Azikiwe. Glad to see a Nigerian made it, even if he “carried last.” What an irony that the same Azikiwe who inspired Nkrumah into Pan-Africanism, while chieftains of his political machine, the NCNC, sheltered Mandela in Nigeria before he went home to be convicted, is mentioned almost as a footnote. For me, the honour is between Mansa Musa, the 14th-century emperor of old Mali, and Selassie, who also reigned as the last emperor of Ethiopia. They were great kings who were second to nobody outside the continent. Mandela’s fame cannot be denied. But from what we see in the South Africa he left behind, it is essentially hype-driven. Mandela was a favourite of the West and its media because of the way he managed South Africa’s transition from apartheid to freedom and democracy without bloodshed in his voluntary five-year, single tenure of office. He created what was referred to as a “rainbow nation” where the former white and black constituents cohabited in … emm … harmony. Here is where the failure shows up: Mandela ended one form of apartheid and, wittingly or unwittingly, installed another form of it. Instead of setting black and white apart racially, he set them apart politically and economically. The black majority, led by Mandela’s African National Congress (ANC), took over political power, which it has monopolised since 1991 when he was installed as the first black president. The whites have gradually retreated from tussling for political control, choosing instead to hold tightly to their share of the booty: the economy and land control. A properly integrated country should have many white politicians vying for and getting elected as presidents inside the ANC, while blacks should be strong economic stakeholders with full access to land. Because of this new apartheid, poverty among the black population has worsened under the ANC. Increasingly, the blacks and whites are once again eyeing each other across an invisible drawn battle line. Indeed, South Africa’s President, Cyril Ramaphosa, in May 2025 had a series of face-offs with President Donald Trump of the United States over alleged white genocide aimed at driving the white people out of South Africa. The main thrusts of Mandela’s failure are three-pronged. He and his ANC leadership failed to groom the South African youth in the ethos of hard work and enterprise to enable them to compete for their fair share of the economic resources of South Africa. The old malaise of laziness, indolence, drunkenness and loose sexual virtues trapped a vast percentage of the youth in poverty and the HIV/AIDS scourge. The end of apartheid also attracted the arrival of other African youths from neighbouring countries and not-so-neighbouring countries such as Nigeria and Ghana. They came with the hard work and enterprise that the South African youths lacked, and before you knew it, the indigenous South Africans started seeing the foreigners as a threat. This feeling was further stoked by the fact that the more affluent foreigners, especially Nigerians, took the best of the stock of South African damsels for themselves. Throughout history, women and economic booty have sparked countless wars. Homer’s Odyssey, from where we derived expressions like “Achilles’ heel,” “Trojan horse” and others, is all about a ten-year war between Greece and Troy over a beautiful lady known as Helen. And so, foreigners in South Africa became the soft, easy targets for enraged indigenous youth. Waves of xenophobic attacks on foreigners have taken over 600 lives since 2008, with the South African law-enforcement agencies failing to act. The government of South Africa and the country’s law-enforcement machinery abandoned the foreigners to the mobs. It is the job of government to enforce the law on illegal migration and alleged crimes, but they give armed rabble free rein to help themselves. For the third time, the Federal Government and some of our international carriers have had to fly hundreds of stranded Nigerians home from South Africa. Mandela and the ANC failed to educate their young people on the role Africans, especially Nigeria, played to support the anti-apartheid struggle. Instead, they surged into Nigeria with their multinational companies such as Multichoice, MTN, Shoprite, Spar and others to gorge on our humongous market while refusing to give our citizens in their country a fair chance to live in safety. That is what we get for our sacrifices for Mandela and the ANC. South African criminal gangs have engaged in orgies of looting of foreigners’ shops and businesses. Unfortunately for them, the erstwhile bustling business districts and markets operated by the deserting foreigners have left the economy in shambles. Is it the lazy, HIV/AIDS-infested drunkards who will now provide the juice the economy needs to come back to life? Let us watch and see how South Africa tides through the lacuna. All I am saying is that Nelson Mandela and the ANC have failed their own people and Africa at large. It was only Mandela and the big politicians who benefitted from the end of apartheid. They left their own people behind. They repaid our good with evil. It is unfortunate that the so-called African Union and its organs exist only in name. One way or the other, South Africa must be made to pay a price for betraying Africa. South Africa, ANC: shame on you! The post Nelson Mandela and ANC’s many failures, by Ochereome Nnanna appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: Taking care of the telecoms poor, by Okoh Aihe
items.description: The holy book tells us that the poor will always live with us in our societies. It means you can never wish away the poor even if you are offended by their existence. No magic wand will obliterate the presence of the poor, except concerted efforts to change their status. Apart from individual efforts, nations and societies are challenged to do something about their poor, to elevate their standard and give them an acceptable modicum of reasonable living. It is common knowledge that nations that fail to take decisive action run the risk of plunging their population into multidimensional poverty. The telecom sector is more civil in this respect, quite urbane in the way it categorises poverty. You will hear expressions like access gap, the digital divide, the unconnected, the unserved and underserved but never anything related to the telecoms poor who have to be accommodated and helped by the system to get connected to the rest of humanity. There is digital poverty all over-within communities, within states and even within nations-but it is never so called because the operators are more careful in using words that can shade their potential customers. Nations and international organisations have continued to make pointed efforts to reduce the communities of the digitally disadvantaged. The other day, the International Telecommunications Union (ITU) announced that commitments under its Partner2Connect (P2C) Digital Coalition have surpassed $100bn, thus “exceeding a milestone in the global effort to expand global Internet connectivity and bridge the digital divide.” Ironically, the UN agency for digital technologies stated that, with one-quarter of the world’s population offline, commitments to Partner2Connect will directly support the goal of achieving universal, meaningful connectivity-ensuring everyone has access to affordable and reliable Internet and the skills to use it safely and effectively. It is not just a pile of cash the ITU is raising through P2C. Some of the pledges will come in kind. For instance, the Asian Development Bank (ADB) has pledged to implement the Asia-Pacific Digital Highway, aiming to mobilise $20bn in public and private investments by 2035 to improve digital connectivity for up to 650 million people across the region through expanded cross-border digital infrastructure, regional digital hubs, and improved broadband access. And part of Microsoft’s $18bn pledge is to advance inclusive digital transformation by connecting more than 450 rural and underserved community hubs in Kenya through satellite-enabled infrastructure integrated with Azure Space. The good news is that Nigeria is very much part of this salutary global effort to accommodate and solve the problems of the digital poor by getting them connected to the footprints of modern technology. Dr Bosun Tijani, Minister of Communications, Innovation and Digital Economy, is a tech evangelist in this respect. As Chair of the 2026 ITU Council, Tijani has tried to mobilise his African colleagues into action to ensure they build enough digital capacity to cover the needs of even the disadvantaged. While welcoming participants to the 7th Ordinary Session of the Conference of Plenipotentiaries-African Telecommunications Union (ITU), Tijani stressed that only cooperation among member states can help enhance regional digital integration, innovation and sustainable economic development across Africa. Working within the global digital coalition to P2C goals for Nigeria, Tijani, encouraged by his boss, President Bola Ahmed Tinubu, has introduced a cocktail of programmes, part of which will derive from commitments by the Nigerian Communications Satellite Limited (NIGCOMSAT). They include the following key targets that are measurable: Local Government Connectivity-connecting all 774 local government secretariats across Nigeria to enhance internet access and promote e-governance; Public Service Infrastructure-broadening coverage to 30,000 Primary Health Centres and 100,000 schools to bridge the rural-urban digital divide; and Satellite Infrastructure Expansion-deploying multi-band and dual-band satellites to expand coverage for the roughly underserved population in sub-Saharan Africa. The minister’s other measurable programmes include: Project BRIDGE-partnering with the International Finance Corporation (IFC) to secure private equity for a $2bn initiative to roll out 90,000 km of open-access fibre optic cable across Nigeria; China’s Connectivity Support-funding from China Industrial Bank and SINOSURE under the Nigeria Universal Access Project (NUCAP) to connect over 20m Nigerians in underdeveloped communities; EU Digital Economy Package; and Digital Villages-collaborating with Globacom and Huawei to establish solar-powered, telemedicine-enabled digital learning hubs in rural communities. A major attraction in what Minister Tijani is doing is that all the programmes are measurable, and it will not be out of place for stakeholders to call for results very soon. The programmes should not be digital illusions but programmes with measurable deliverables that can immediately affect and impact the people. In terms of programmes that alleviate the plight of the telecom poor, the Nigerian Communications Commission (NCC) has equally initiated quite a number. Plus the activities of the Universal Service Provision Fund (USPF) and the Digital Bridge Institute (DBI), which itself is an institution dedicated to building digital literacy and tech knowledge acquisition, the regulator has embarked on activities that can enrich the industry in the long run. For instance, universal access has remained a key programme of the regulator. Keynoting the ATU meeting in Abuja, Dr Aminu Maida, Executive Vice Chairman, NCC, noted that “the issues before us are increasingly complex and interconnected: spectrum harmonisation across borders, the governance of artificial intelligence and autonomous systems; data protection frameworks that can support African digital trade; universal access in communities where commercial investment alone may not be sufficient; and cybersecurity threats that cross national boundaries.” Noting that no administration can address these challenges effectively in isolation, he appealed that “our regulatory cooperation must therefore become more continuous, more technical and more institutionalised.” The 7th Ordinary Session of the Conference Preparatory Committee of the African Telecommunications Union provides an opportunity for African countries to take a common position in readiness for the ITU PP-26, which holds in Doha before the end of the year. Only recently, the regulator called its Second Stakeholders Forum on Nigeria’s Dig-Once Policy, which is expected to reduce the headache and cost of fibre optic cable deployment across the nation. The Dig-Once Policy Initiative is designed to reduce the cost, duplication, and complexity of network deployment by encouraging coordinated civil works and shared infrastructure. Speaking on the subject, Dimension Data’s Olugbenga Olabiyi observed that “For Nigeria, where broadband expansion remains a national priority under the National Broadband Plan, successful implementation of the Dig-Once Policy could become one of the most impactful infrastructure reforms in our telecommunications history. Every reduction in fibre deployment costs strengthens the business case for extending connectivity to schools, hospitals, government institutions, businesses, and underserved communities,” he declared. While one would be the first to acknowledge the plethora of challenges confronting the telecommunications industry, it would be remiss not to equally acknowledge that such challenges hardly invalidate the efforts of the stakeholders to float programmes that benefit the digital poor. The sector leaves nobody behind. That is where the word inclusivity comes from. The primary objective of Partner2Connect cements the care and concern for the digital poor, which is to connect the hardest-to-reach communities-particularly in Least Developed Countries (LDCs), Landlocked Developing Countries (LLDCs), and Small Island Developing States (SIDS). Quite some aspirational language which covers even the telecom poor and gives everybody hope. The post Taking care of the telecoms poor, by Okoh Aihe appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: Why the NBA physician must first heal herself, by Rotimi Fasan
items.description: With the emergence of Oyinkansola Badejo-Okusanya (SAN) as the 33rd president of the Nigerian Bar Association, the controversies that had preceded this year’s election would seem to have subsided despite fears that the post-election period might be mired in litigation. Such fears have so far proven only partially founded, as some lawyers have allegedly headed for the courts while the losers in the presidential contest, Lateef Omoyemi Akangba (SAN) and Olumuyiwa Akinboro (SAN), seem to be taking the outcome of the election in their strides. The newly elected president has extended her hand of friendship to the two other contestants. Mrs. Badejo-Okusanya will not be the first woman to occupy the office of the president when the transition period ends and Mazi Afam Osigwe formally hands over to her. That distinction belongs to Dame Priscilla Kuye, who became president to complete the second of the two-year term of Mr. Clement Akpamgbo (SAN), who had left the NBA presidency after a year to assume office as the Attorney-General of the Federation under the military regime of General Ibrahim Babangida. But Oyinkansola Badejo-Okusanya is the first female to occupy the office of NBA president by election, and while she appears to have been a very popular candidate, her victory, or the processes that made it possible, have thrown up questions that speak to issues well beyond her immediate constituency as a legal practitioner. It has highlighted the fault lines that have undermined the authority of the NBA as a moral arbiter and a supposedly impartial advocate within the larger context of Nigerian politics. It was just over a year ago, precisely in April 2025, that the NBA was caught in the controversy of a N300 million largess the Rivers State government gave the association for the ‘hosting rights’ of the NBA conference that was to be held in the state. The NBA decided to relocate its conference to Enugu and denied Rivers the hosting right following the declaration of emergency rule in the state. The NBA, whose motto is to champion ‘the rule of law,’ was disgusted by what it viewed as President Bola Tinubu’s despotic overreach and threw its weight behind the suspended governor, Siminalayi Fubara. But the state government under Vice Admiral Ibok-Ete Ibas, the Sole Administrator, demanded that the NBA return its N300 million. Like the chichidodo bird that despises maggot but feeds on excreta, the NBA made a great show of its displeasure with Abuja for its supposed disregard of the rule of law but firmly held on to the N300 million conference support fund. The NBA called the money a grant, but it could not tell anyone what it was for. When INEC deregistered the ADC in April this year (a decision that has been upheld by the Appeal Court), the outgoing NBA president, Afam Osigwe, came out shooting from the hip as he took the judiciary, particularly judges, to task for their perceived support of the executive in its purported drive towards a one-party state. We are in July, the 2027 election is yet about six months away, and there are at least twenty registered, INEC-recognised, political parties in Nigeria as I write this. But one of the most contentious issues on which the NBA (or is it its leadership?) has been prominently critical concerns the transmission of election results. Many lawyers, including those in the NBA leadership, have gone out on a limb to support the opposition parties’ call for ‘real-time electronic transfer’ of election results. Nothing anyone said about the dangers that constituted made any sense to either the opposition politicians or their NBA supporters, who as public commentators, media analysts and pundits excoriated the ruling party, the executive, INEC and the National Assembly that upheld the decision to marry both the manual and digital models of election result management. The opposition parties are still breathing fire and brimstone on the matter. But the emptiness of their position was made visible the moment the time came for the political parties to produce both the digital and paper registers of their membership ahead of the party primaries in May. Suddenly, the advocates of real-time electronic transfer of results became dumb as they stumbled from pillar to post looking for a way out of the registration. They had been caught flat-footed and unable to register their members within the stipulated deadline. It was not until the deadline was extended, following judicial interventions, that the parties were able to save face and produce their membership registers. That was the same embarrassing situation that the NBA was thrown into when its 2026 election that produced the NBA president-elect was stalled for several hours after the election portal experienced a technical glitch that locked out many of its members from participating in the election. There were multiple reports of cyber-attacks and a total breakdown of electoral capacity for several hours after the election was supposed to have started. Many lawyers were not able to log into the voting portal or voted without their votes being counted. It was chaos that had been foretold but which the NBA leadership chose to ignore, only to produce a result that has cast an unnecessary and avoidable shadow over the victory of a candidate that many believed would still have won had the election been delayed to attend to the pitfalls identified in the weeks leading to the election. This was an election of just over 82,000 participants, yet the loud-mouthed NBA and its ever-litigious members that are quick to ‘approach’ the courts for the judicial interpretation of the most transparent issues have lost their voice. Yet they would sit in judgment over other Nigerians and make generalised claims about a country of 250 million people, 100 million of whom are eligible to vote, based on their experience of 100,000 lawyers. In the end only 26,184 of the 82,172 registered voters participated in the NBA presidential election. The winner, with 12,317 votes, got 47.18 per cent of the votes cast for the presidential election. Lateef Omoyemi Akangba polled 7,934 votes (30.39 per cent) and Olumuyiwa Akinboro polled 5,855 votes (22.43 per cent) to emerge, respectively, as the first and second runners-up. Now, the NBA is asking for understanding – the type it never extended to the winners of the 2023 presidential election or to INEC, on whom they heaped all the blame for the technical glitch that affected the 2023 election. They are not telling us that turnout for their election was low or that the combined votes of the losers outstripped those of the winner in order to delegitimise the result or, in fact, establish how unpopular the winner is. All is silent, and the NBA craves our understanding. I fear who no fear lawyers! The post Why the NBA physician must first heal herself, by Rotimi Fasan appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: Donald Trump annonce des droits de douane de 100 % sur les médicaments génériques à partir de 2028
items.description: À partir d'août 2028, les États-Unis imposeront des droits de douane de 100 % sur les médicaments génériques, a annoncé le président Donald Trump mardi. Objectif : relocaliser la production des produits pharmaceutiques sur le territoire américain.

items.title: Affaire OGP: ce que révèlent les relevés bancaires sur le différend Faramayah Autos et l’ex-direction
items.description: Après plusieurs jours d'audition et dix jours passés en garde à vue, l'ancien directeur général de l'Office guinéen de publicité (OGP), Aladji Cellou Camara, a été placé sous mandat de dépôt. Cette décision intervient dans le cadre d'une nouvelle procédure judiciaire ouverte dans le prolongement de l'affaire dite « MTN ». Dans ce dossier, largement médiatisé, il avait pourtant bénéficié d'un placement sous contrôle judiciaire le 6 juillet dernier. Mais, manifestement insatisfait du non placement de l’ancien directeur général de l’Office guinéen de publicité (OGP) sous mandat de dépôt, le Parquet spécial a décidé d’engager, trois jours plus tard, une procédure de flagrant délit à son encontre. Une démarche qui apparaît, selon des observateurs, comme le seul moyen pour le parquet spécial de conduire Aladji Cellou en détention. L’ancien directeur général de l’OGP devait ainsi être présenté, dès la prochaine audience, devant un juge afin de débattre du fond du dossier. Initialement orienté ainsi en procédure de flagrant délit par le parquet spécial près la Cour de répression des infractions économiques et financières (CRIEF), le dossier a finalement été renvoyé devant la Chambre de l'instruction pour complément d'enquête. Enterrant ainsi le flagrant délit. Les juristes consultés au sujet de cette volte-face du parquet n'y voient qu'une seule explication : il fallait coûte que coûte placer Aladji Cellou en détention. Car, affirment-ils, «en matière d'enquête financière, le flagrant délit ne peut jamais prospérer, sauf à tordre le coup à la Loi.» Une fois devant la chambre d'instruction, celle-ci, selon des avocats proches du dossier, aurait été « contrainte » de valider le placement en détention ordonné quelques jours plus tôt par le parquet. Abracadabrantesque ! Au fur et à mesure des investigations, plusieurs sources proches du dossier affirment qu'aucun élément ne mettrait, à ce stade, en évidence un détournement de deniers publics. Selon les mêmes sources, il n'est pas reproché à l'ancien directeur général Aladji Cellou d'avoir soustrait les ressources de l'OGP ou vidé les comptes de l'entreprise. Les faits examinés par les enquêteurs porteraient davantage sur le règlement ou non de dettes héritées de la précédente gestion, notamment celles contractées avant son arrivée à la tête de l'OGP. Une entreprise lourdement endettée voire en faillite totale à l’arrivée de l’équipe d’Aladji Cellou Lorsque Aladji Cellou Camara prenait les commandes de l'OGP, l'entreprise publique faisait face à plus de 80 milliards de francs guinéens de dettes, selon les documents de passation de service, dont 52 milliards de dettes fiscales qui se sont accumulées, selon nos sources depuis l'ère Capi Camara. En l'espace de 18 mois, près de 14 milliards de francs guinéens auraient été consacrés au remboursement d'anciennes dettes, environ 15 milliards reversés au Trésor public au titre des impôts, près de 35 milliards affectés au paiement des salaires et environ 13 milliards de francs guinéens demeuraient disponibles dans les comptes de l'entreprise à la fin de sa gestion. Nos sources indiquent que l'équipe d'Aladji Cellou est la seule à n'avoir pas endetté l'OGP, y compris vis-à-vis des impôts, depuis belle lurette. Le différend avec Faramayah Autos Parmi les dossiers qui retiennent l'attention, figure celui opposant l'ancien directeur général Mandian Sidibé à Ansoumane Camara, PDG de Faramayah Autos, fournisseur de véhicules de l'OGP. Joint par téléphone, ce dernier affirme avoir livré six véhicules sous la direction de Mandian Sidibé (trois Toyota Hilux, deux Fortuner et un Land Cruiser Prado) pour un montant total de 3,65 milliards de francs guinéens. « À ce jour, je n'ai reçu que 500 millions de francs guinéens », soutient-il, rejetant les informations selon lesquelles son entreprise aurait déjà perçu plus de trois milliards de francs. Selon lui, un protocole d'accord avait été conclu avec Aladji Cellou Camara afin d'apurer progressivement la créance. « On a fait un protocole d'accord. Il s'était engagé à me payer 250 millions de francs par mois. Il m'a payé deux tranches de 250 millions, soit 500 millions. Ensuite, il m'a expliqué qu'il rencontrait des difficultés parce que Mandian avait laissé beaucoup de dettes », raconte Ansoumane Camara. Le fournisseur affirme avoir ensuite entrepris plusieurs démarches, sans succès, avant de saisir un huissier de justice. Seulement voilà : quand Aladji Cellou arrivait à l'OGP, de tous les véhicules cités plus haut, il n'y avait que trois : la Prado (complètement amorti), du DG, la Fortuner du DGA et un pick-up Hilux en très mauvais état, les PV en font foi. Où sont passés les autres véhicules ? Une confrontation entre les différentes parties à la justice devra permettre d’apporter clairement une réponse à cette question. Face à cette situation, des relevés bancaires ont parlé et contredisent totalement la version du fournisseur Toutefois, des documents bancaires consultés dans le cadre du dossier, présentent une version différente. Ils font état de plusieurs paiements effectués au profit de Faramayah Autos. D'abord Aladji Cellou a effectué trois virements pour un total de 800 millions, contrairement aux 500 millions reconnus par Ansoumane Camara. Mais il y a pire : on découvre que le fournisseur avait reçu de nombreux autres chèques à partir du compte de l'OGP logé au Trésor public. Selon ces relevés, un premier chèque n°6359976 d'un montant de 820 millions de francs guinéens a été émis le 19 juillet 2022 en faveur de Faramayah Autos. Le 8 août 2022, trois autres chèques ont été enregistrés : le chèque n°6386901 de 500 millions de francs guinéens, le chèque n°6386902, également de 500 millions de francs guinéens, et le chèque n°6386700 de 300 millions de francs guinéens. Les relevés bancaires font également apparaître d'autres décaissements intervenus le 10 janvier 2023. Il s'agit notamment du chèque n°6392596 d'un montant de 490 millions de francs guinéens, du chèque n°6392597 de 480 millions de francs guinéens et du chèque n°6399714 de 475 millions de francs guinéens, tous établis au profit de Faramayah Autos SARL. Au total, ces différentes opérations représentent 3 565 000 000 GNF (3,565 milliards de francs guinéens). Il convient de préciser que ces paiements ont été effectués sous la direction de Mandian Sidibé, période durant laquelle Faramayah Autos avait livré les six véhicules à l'OGP. Pourtant, Ansoumane Camara continue de réclamer à l'équipe d'Aladji Cellou Camara le paiement de 3,650 milliards de francs guinéens, affirmant n'avoir perçu, à ce jour, que 500 millions de francs guinéens. Cette contradiction entre les déclarations du fournisseur et les mouvements bancaires constitue l'un des points que les enquêteurs de la CRIEF devraient examiner afin de déterminer à quoi correspondent les 3,565 milliards de francs guinéens versés par l'OGP à Faramayah Autos. Dans ce dossier, il est également question d'une avance sur salaire obtenue par l'ancien directeur général de l'OGP, Mandian Sidibé, auprès d'une banque de la place. Selon les éléments consultés, alors que l'entreprise accusait plusieurs mois d'arriérés de salaires, Mandian Sidibé avait sollicité, en octobre 2024, une avance bancaire adossée à son salaire mensuel ainsi qu'aux rappels de salaires qui lui étaient dus, afin de faire face à des charges familiales. Dans une correspondance consultée par Guinéenews, la banque indique que le dernier salaire effectivement crédité sur le compte de Mandian Sidibé avant son départ de l'OGP était celui du mois d'octobre 2024. L'établissement précise qu'il s'attendait au versement des rémunérations des mois de novembre et décembre, au même titre que celles des autres employés. « Malheureusement, tel n'a pas été le cas », relève l'institution financière dans ce document. D’après nos informations, ces réalités n’ont pas suffi pour dissuader l'avocat de Mandian Sidibé d'écrire le 5 février 2025 à son successeur à la tête de l’OGP, Aladji Cellou pour réclamer les mêmes arriérés de salaires. À travers deux virements, a-t-on appris, Aladji Cellou se serait acquitté de « cette obligation salariale », avant de recevoir le courrier de la banque auprès de laquelle Mandian Sidibé a endetté l'OGP à hauteur de 3 milliards de francs guinéens. Sur cet autre montant, suivant une résolution du Conseil d’Administration et un protocole d'accord, Aladji Cellou a payé 500 millions de francs guinéens. Contacté par Guinéenews, l’avocat de Mandian Sidibé, Me. Sékou Traoré indique n'avoir eu connaissance ni du courrier adressé à Aladji Cellou Camara au sujet des arriérés de salaire de Mandian Sidibé, ni de la correspondance transmise à la banque concernant l'avance sur salaire. Une interrogation brûle désormais toutes les lèvres : pourquoi celui qui a contracté les dettes en l’occurrence Mandian Sidibé, a-t-il été libéré, tandis que son successeur, Aladji Cellou, qui a hérité des dettes de gestion et s’est attelé à les apurer sans en créer de nouvelles, se retrouve derrière les barreaux ? Une équation qui alimente les débats et reste, pour l’heure, sans véritable réponse. Et c’est là effectivement où la CRIEF est attendue pour tirer cette rocambolesque affaire au clair. Nous y reviendrons.

items.title: Villa in talks to sign Chelsea’s Garnacho
items.description: Aston Villa are in advanced talks to sign Alejandro Garnacho on loan, with a potential obligation to buy. The Argentina winger, 21, was made available for sale by Chelsea for £43m last month, but the club are open to a move that could see him leave permanently, under certain conditions, even if it initially takes the form of a loan. It follows Chelsea signing Morgan Rogers for a record £117m fee from Villa on Tuesday night. If finalised, Garnacho would be Villa’s fourth signing in a busy summer of incomings and outgoings, after Johan Manzambi, Joao Gomes and Modou Keba Cisse moved to the Midlands club. Player sales out of Villa total nearly £200m with Youri Tielemans, Donyell Malen, Enzo Barrenechea and Lewis Dobbin all leaving the club. The Blues have been keen to offload Garnacho despite signing him only last September in a £40m deal from Manchester United , who would be due 10 per cent of any eventual transfer fee. His impact at Stamford Bridge has been limited to one Premier League goal, and the arrival of Rogers and Sporting winger Geovany Quenda have increased the need to reduce the size of the squad.

items.title: TPAME salue le lancement de la campagne nationale "Manskoutch"
items.description: L’Association Touche Pas à Mon Enfant (TPAME) a indiqué avoir pris connaissance « avec intérêt » du lancement de la première campagne nationale de lutte contre les violences faites aux enfants, sous le slogan +Manskoutch+ (Nous ne nous tairons pas). Dans un communiqué, elle a salué cette initiative nationale, qui constitue, selon elle, « une avancée positive pour renforcer la culture de la prévention, du signalement et de la protection des enfants contre toutes les formes de violence, d’exploitation et de négligence ». L’Association souligne toutefois que le succès de cette campagne « ne se mesurera pas uniquement à la diffusion de ses messages de sensibilisation, mais surtout à sa capacité à produire un impact concret sur le terrain ». « Cela passe notamment par le renforcement des mécanismes de signalement, l’amélioration de la prise en charge des enfants victimes, ainsi que la mise en place d’un accompagnement psychologique, social et juridique adapté, dans le cadre d’une coordination efficace entre l’ensemble des acteurs concernés », estime l'ONG. Touche Pas à Mon Enfant appelle également à une implication pleine et entière des organisations de la société civile spécialisées, en tant que partenaires essentiels dans la mise en œuvre de cette stratégie, en valorisant leur expertise de terrain en matière de prévention, de sensibilisation, de détection et d’accompagnement. Enfin, l’Association rappelle que la protection des enfants est une responsabilité collective qui incombe à la famille, à l’école, aux institutions, aux médias et à la société civile. Pour conclure que « le slogan +Manskoutch+ doit se traduire par un engagement permanent à ne jamais garder le silence face à une quelconque forme de violence exercée contre un enfant ». The post TPAME salue le lancement de la campagne nationale "Manskoutch" appeared first on Hespress Français - Actualités du Maroc .

items.title: Keeper Martinez may ‘step aside’ from Argentina
items.description: Argentina goalkeeper Emi Martinez says he is considering whether it is “time to step aside” from international football after his side’s 1-0 defeat to Spain in the World Cup final. The 33-year-old has won 67 caps for his country and played every minute of Argentina’s eight World Cup games, keeping two clean sheets and conceding eight goals. He was a World Cup winner in 2022, also claiming the golden glove award as the tournament’s outstanding goalkeeper. “I dreamt we’d win it again, I dreamt of bringing it back to Argentina and making history once more,” Martinez wrote in a post on Instagram. “The truth is, the pain is hard to explain. There are many things to reflect on, including how to move forward and whether it’s time to step aside. “I’m so sorry, I truly tried my best to help my country and my teammates.” Martinez won the Yashin Trophy , awarded to the world’s best goalkeeper at the Ballon d’Or awards, in 2023 and 2024. He has played for Aston Villa since 2020 and has a contract until 2029 , but has been linked to a move to Italian side Juventus this summer.

items.title: Infantino’s ‘time is up’ with Fifa – La Liga boss
items.description: La Liga president Javier Tebas has called for FIFA president Gianni Infantino to step down, claiming the world governing body is “destroying the football industry”. In an interview with Italian newspaper La Gazzetta dello Sport, external , the head of the Spanish league also criticised World Cup hydration breaks, the final’s lengthy half-time interval, the postponement of a suspension for United States striker Folarin Balogun, and the future possibility of a 64-team summer tournament. When asked if Infantino should quit, Tebas said: “In my opinion, yes, I think his time is up.” However, he stated that the 56-year-old has the support of national federations, and claimed FIFA’s election system is “rotten at its core”. “There’s no opposition candidate, nobody wants to stand just to lose,” Tebas said. “He shouldn’t stay, but the current state of affairs means he won’t leave.” The head of Spain’s top flight was in the US to see his country win the 2026 World Cup. “These past few days here in America, I’ve heard many people who are against Infantino, who disagree with what he’s doing,” Tebas said. “They say it, but then they do nothing. I don’t know what’s worse, the silence or the complicity, because those who stay silent are perfectly aware of the damage football is suffering.” Infantino is expected to be re-elected for a fourth term at Fifa’s March congress, although Tebas believes Infantino could have been brought down as a consequence of his involvement in the case around Balogun. The 25-year-old US player escaped a suspension for his side’s last-16 tie with Belgium following an intervention from US President Donald Trump. “The suspension of the American player’s ban was absolutely serious. They were lucky that Belgium eliminated the USA, because otherwise a case could have arisen that might have cost Infantino his job,” Tebas said. “Since Belgium won, they managed to bury the matter. But these things are just the tip of the iceberg.” European football’s governing body Uefa criticised Fifa’s decision at the time, calling it “unprecedented, incomprehensible and unjustifiable”, and the decision was a factor in the organisation’s president Aleksander Ceferin not attending the World Cup final. Infantino opened the door to a 64-team global tournament during this year’s event, but Tebas argued that “not everything can revolve around the World Cup”. “Increasing the number of national teams makes no sense. The football industry isn’t just the World Cup,” Tebas said. “It’s the national competitions that sustain this sport. They are destroying the football industry, which generates tens of thousands of jobs, for an event that lasts 40 days and involves a minority of players. “Are the best players there? Clearly, but they are also in our leagues. We need fewer international matches and more protection for national football, at all levels. They don’t realise they are making irresponsible decisions.” FIFA brought in mandatory three-minute hydration breaks for the World Cup, regardless of conditions, for all matches played at stadiums in the United States, Mexico and Canada. The governing body said they were brought in as a commitment to player welfare, but critics have pointed to broadcasters using the breaks as a way to cash in with commercials. Meanwhile, pop music giants Madonna, BTS, Shakira and Justin Bieber were among the acts to perform in the World Cup final half-time show , which extended the usual 15-minute half-time break to 27 minutes 22 seconds. “FIFA arranges things as it pleases, for what it wants, for its own interests, certainly not for football,” said Tebas. “So, if they need a 27-minute half-time break, they’ll have it. “Hydration breaks are a lie. We have them in La Liga, but only when it’s genuinely hot. “Here, the pitches in Dallas, Los Angeles, Atlanta had air conditioning; I had to put on a jumper in the stands. It was a lie, a break for advertising. “It’s proof that we are governed by an institution that does and decides what it wants, when it wants, following only its own interests without considering the rest of football. And in many cases, it acts to the detriment of football.” BBC Sport has contacted FIFA for comment.

items.title: CAN 2032 : Le trio AES, Burkina-Mali-Niger officiellement dans la course à l’organisation
items.description: C'est une offensive diplomatique et sportive d'envergure que vient de lancer l'espace sahélien. Dans un communiqué officiel publié ce 20 juillet 2026, la Fédération nigérienne de football (FENIFOOT) a annoncé que le Burkina Faso, le Mali et le Niger sont officiellement candidats à la co-organisation de la Coupe d’Afrique des Nations (CAN) 2032. Cette déclaration d'intérêt, validée par la Confédération africaine de football (CAF), marque le début d'un processus ambitieux porté par les pays de l'Alliance des États du Sahel (AES). Entre volonté de coopération régionale et désir de rayonnement international, les trois nations s'engagent désormais dans une course contre la montre pour bâtir un dossier de candidature aux standards mondiaux. La fumée blanche est venue de Niamey, confirmant une rumeur qui agitait les milieux sportifs depuis quelques semaines. Les fédérations de football du Burkina Faso, du Mali et du Niger ont franchi une étape décisive en déposant officiellement une déclaration d’intérêt conjointe pour l’organisation de la CAN 2032, conformément aux règlements de la CAF. Le lancement de cette candidature a pris une dimension solennelle le 18 juillet 2026 à New York. En marge de rencontres internationales, une réunion de travail capitale s'est tenue entre le président de la CAF, le Dr Patrice Motsepe, et les chefs des fédérations concernées : le Colonel Oumarou Sawadogo (Burkina Faso), Monsieur Mahazou Cisset (Mali) et Monsieur Issaka Adamou (Niger), en présence du membre du Conseil de la FIFA, le Colonel-Major Djibrilla Hima Hamidou. Au cours de cet entretien, le patron du football africain a pris acte de cette « ambition commune » et a salué une initiative fondée sur une vision de coopération régionale. Dr Motsepe a encouragé les trois États à poursuivre leurs efforts tout en respectant scrupuleusement les exigences procédurales de la CAF pour la suite du processus. Cette démarche ne se limite pas au simple cadre sportif. Elle s’inscrit dans la dynamique de l’Alliance des États du Sahel (AES), dont les autorités nationales soutiennent activement ce projet. Pour la FENIFOOT, cette avancée témoigne de la volonté des trois nations de mutualiser leurs atouts et leurs capacités afin d'offrir au continent une compétition de haut niveau. L'entrée dans le processus de candidature est désormais acquise. Toutefois, le plus dur reste à faire. Les fédérations des trois pays entendent poursuivre leur étroite collaboration pour la préparation du dossier de candidature détaillé. Ce document devra répondre aux critères stricts de la CAF en matière d'infrastructures sportives, de transport, d'hébergement et de sécurité. En réaffirmant leur détermination à contribuer à la réussite de cette « ambition historique », le Burkina Faso, le Mali et le Niger placent le sport comme un levier de solidarité et de développement pour le Sahel de demain. L’article CAN 2032 : Le trio AES, Burkina-Mali-Niger officiellement dans la course à l'organisation est apparu en premier sur NetAfrique.net .

items.title: Japan seeks Southeast Asia sea lanes to avoid troubled waters in region
items.description: Japan is turning to five lesser-used straits in Southeast Asia as insurance against the next maritime shock, with plans to create detailed nautical charts for ships to navigate through these waters and bring vital goods to its economy. Tokyo would work with Indonesia and the Philippines to chart their respective straits, the foreign ministry told the Yomiuri newspaper, as it stepped up efforts to mitigate disruption to global trade arising from the ongoing crisis in the Strait of Hormuz.
